_comment: Draft content for wh40k-11e. Merge into rules-v1.json after PDF verification. Not loaded by app.

gameSystemPatch.name: Warhammer 40,000
gameSystemPatch.tagline: New to 40k or upgrading from 10th? Guided setup and table play

externalLinks.title: Chapter Approved Deck — How It Works
Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon — What's in the Box

gettingStartedSteps.title: What You Need
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Miniatures, dice, a measuring tool, terrain, and the free core rules.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Warhammer 40,000 is a two-player miniatures wargame. You need an army of models, six-sided dice, a tape measure (inches), a battlefield with terrain, and the rules. The 11th Edition core rules are free on Warhammer Community. The Armageddon launch box is an optional starter with Space Marines, Orks, mission cards, and a rulebook — about 1,000 points per side.
tips: You do not need every codex on day one — your existing 10th Edition codex still works in 11th.
Paint models for the Battle Ready bonus in matched play (+10 VP).

gettingStartedSteps.title: Build Your First Army
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Pick a faction, a points limit, and spend Detachment Points on detachments.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Choose an Army Faction and a points limit — Incursion (1,000 pts) is a good first game; Strike Force (2,000 pts) is standard. Instead of one detachment, you spend Detachment Points (2 at 1k, 3 at 2k) on one or more detachments. Each detachment adds rules, stratagems, and a Force Disposition that shapes your missions. Attach Leaders and Support Characters to units when you write the list, not on the table.
tips: Check the Munitorum Field Manual for points and which characters can join which units.
At 2,000 points most armies end up with six or nine stratagems depending on detachments chosen.

gettingStartedSteps.title: Set Up the Battlefield
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Terrain footprints are objectives — not just circles on a mat.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Place terrain before deployment. In 11th Edition, key locations are often terrain footprints — ruins, fortifications, or areas on the board — rather than round objective markers. Use the Chapter Approved mission deck (in the Armageddon box or as a separate product) to pick deployment and missions from your armies' Force Dispositions.
tips: Dense terrain (ruins, woods) interacts with cover, Hidden, and objective control.
Casual play can use simplified terrain maps from the mission deck.

gettingStartedSteps.title: How a Turn Works
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Command, Move, Shoot, Charge, Fight — then score.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Each battle round, players alternate turns. On your turn you run five phases in order: Command (battle-shock tests, stratagems, abilities), Movement, Shooting, Charge, and Fight. Pass the device when your turn ends. Victory points are scored from primary missions, secondary objectives, and the Battle Ready bonus — mission details come from your Chapter Approved cards.
tips: Battle-shock tests happen in the Command phase — not only when a unit first breaks.
Overwatch fires at the end of the enemy Movement phase, not during Charges.

gettingStartedSteps.title: How Fighting Works
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Hit, wound, save, damage — now resolved in fast batches.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Each attack uses the target's Ballistic Skill (shooting) or Weapon Skill (melee). Roll to hit, then to wound comparing Strength to Toughness, then the defender rolls saves. A wound fails only if it fails both the Armour save (after AP) and the Invulnerable save — invulnerable saves are not optional. Failed saves inflict damage; remove models when wounds are exceeded. Identical attacks are rolled together; the defender sets wound allocation order before saves.
tips: Cover imposes -1 BS on the shooter — not +1 to your armour save.
Engagement range is 2 inches for charges and melee.

gettingStartedSteps.title: How You Win
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Missions follow your army's Force Disposition.
gettingStartedSteps.body: Before the game, each player picks a Force Disposition tied to their detachments — Take and Hold, Purge the Foe, Reconnaissance, Disruption, or Priority Assets. Your disposition and your opponent's determine the mission matchup from the Chapter Approved deck. Primary and secondary objectives can differ between players. In matched play, each player can score up to 45 VP from primaries and up to 45 VP from secondaries over the game, with a maximum of 15 VP from each per battle round.
tips: Tournament players usually lock one Force Disposition for the event.
Twist cards are optional for casual games.

gettingStartedSteps.title: Play Your First Game
gettingStartedSteps.summary: Start with Incursion and lean on the rules reference at the table.
gettingStartedSteps.body: For a first 11th Edition game, try a 1,000-point Incursion using the Armageddon armies or your own lists. Read Getting Started, keep the rules reference handy on this device, and use the official core rules PDF for full detail. Guided Match for 40k is coming in a future app update.
tips: New to wargaming? Read this guide, then try What's New if a friend played 10th Edition.
Expect the first few games to be slower — Fight phase sequencing is the biggest habit change.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Your Codexes Still Work
editionMigrationSteps.summary: 10th Edition codexes and detachments remain legal.
editionMigrationSteps.body: 11th Edition is an evolution, not a full reboot. Your existing codexes, faction rules, and most campaign supplement detachments remain valid. Over 70 new or updated detachments launch with the edition, but you are not forced to rebuy faction books on day one.
tips: Download your faction's #New40k Faction Pack from Warhammer Community for updated detachment points and dispositions.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Modular Detachments
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Spend Detachment Points on one or more detachments.
editionMigrationSteps.body: You no longer pick exactly one detachment. At 2,000 points you typically have three Detachment Points to spend on detachments costing one to three points each. Cheaper detachments offer narrower rules and fewer stratagems; expensive ones affect more of your army. You cannot take two detachments sharing the same keyword. Unused points give no bonus.
tips: Enhancement limits still apply — four at 2,000 points, two at 1,000.
List construction now includes attaching Leaders and Support Characters to specific units.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Missions Follow Your Army
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Force Dispositions replace picking Mission Card A.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Each detachment ties to a Force Disposition — Take and Hold, Purge the Foe, Reconnaissance, Disruption, or Priority Assets. You choose one disposition per battle (locked in events). Your disposition and your opponent's determine which of 15 mission matchups you play, including deployment and primary objectives. You and your opponent may be scoring different primaries.
tips: Secondaries: draw two in your Command phase each turn; score up to 15 VP from secondaries per battle round (45 VP max per game).
Primary missions also cap at 15 VP per battle round (45 VP max per game).

editionMigrationSteps.title: Terrain Is the Objective
editionMigrationSteps.body: Round objective markers are gone. You contest and control terrain footprints — ruins, strongpoints, and marked areas on the board. Objective Secured is in the core rules. More models can fit a footprint than an old 40mm circle, so Objective Control values matter more.
tips: Your old objective tokens still work as table markers if you agree with your opponent — but rules refer to terrain.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Cover & Hidden
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Cover hurts the shooter's BS; units can hide in ruins.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Benefit of Cover is now -1 Ballistic Skill to the firing unit, not +1 to your armour save. Infantry, Beasts, and Swarms in a terrain area with at least one Light or Dense feature can become Hidden if the unit has not made any ranged attacks this turn or last turn and is beyond the enemy's Detection Range (usually 15 inches). Hidden units cannot be targeted unless the shooter is close enough. AP-1 weapons bite harder into cover-granting targets.
tips: Vehicles and Monsters generally need obscurement, not footprints, for cover.
Plunging Fire: +1 BS when shooting from terrain at least 3 inches tall.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Charge: Roll First, Target After
editionMigrationSteps.body: Declare a charge if an enemy is within 12 inches, roll 2D6, then choose enemy unit(s) you can reach with a charge move. Engagement range is now 2 inches. You need not base models on enemies — ending within 1 inch of targets if possible is the goal. You may decline to move after a good roll if every reachable target is unappealing.
tips: Successful charges grant Fights First — but the active player still picks which Fights First unit goes first.
Deep Strike charges still need 9+ on 2D6 where setup must be more than 8 inches away.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Fight Phase Overhaul
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Pile-in together, active player activates first, consolidate at the end.
editionMigrationSteps.body: All pile-in moves happen before any fights resolve. The active player selects the first unit to fight (including Fights First). Players alternate until done. Overrun Fights give an extra pile-in if a unit is eligible but not engaged when selected. After all fights, eligible units consolidate 3 inches in a shared step. Supporting Attacks are gone — 2 inch engagement range only.
tips: Fights First is weaker than in 10th because chargers on the active turn swing before defensive Fights First.
Watch for consolidation pulling new enemy units into combat.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Sticky Battle-shock
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Test at half strength; pass in Command to recover.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Units at or below Half-strength test Battle-shock in the Command phase. Battle-shocked units set Objective Control to a null value ("-"), cannot be targeted by Stratagems, and cannot perform Actions. Shock does not clear automatically — you must pass a test in a later Command phase. Insane Bravery still auto-passes one test per game but not for a unit already Battle-shocked.
tips: Forcing extra Battle-shock tests on an already shocked unit is more valuable than in 10th.
Chaplains and similar effects that remove Battle-shock are more important.

editionMigrationSteps.title: Shooting & Overwatch
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Batch saves, indirect nerfs, Overwatch at end of Movement.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Defenders group models by save profile and allocate lowest save dice first — characters take spillover last. Indirect Fire is weaker: cover to target, no hit re-rolls, and usually hit only on 6+ without a stationary spotter. Overwatch triggers once at the end of the enemy Movement phase (not after each move or in the Charge phase) and cannot re-roll hits.
tips: Torrent weapons are strong Overwatch picks.
Close-quarters shooting while engaged penalises non-Close-quarters weapons.

editionMigrationSteps.title: List Building Tweaks
editionMigrationSteps.summary: 9 inch coherency, free rotation, attached characters.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Unit coherency requires every model to be within 9 inches of every other model in the unit — wide conga lines are harder. Models rotate for free during moves and can move through friendly units. Leaders and Support Characters attach during list building; if the bodyguard is destroyed, the character remains the same unit for resurrection effects. Warlord must match your Army Faction keyword.
tips: Some Enhancements are Upgrades on non-characters — still one Enhancement per unit.
Invulnerable saves are no longer optional when taking saves.

editionMigrationSteps.title: What's Not in the Free Core Yet
editionMigrationSteps.summary: Legends, Crusade in codexes, full app-only construction detail.
editionMigrationSteps.body: Legends rules move to the Warhammer 40,000 app and are not in the launch core bundle. Crusade rules are not in new codexes — the Dominatus narrative deck in Armageddon replaces that lane for now. Full army construction detail and some keyword definitions live in the app and Munitorum Field Manual alongside faction packs.
tips: Link out to official downloads for points and detachment metadata.
Tabletome summarizes core play — not a replacement for GW datacards.

ruleSections.title: 11th Edition Overview
ruleSections.content: Warhammer 40,000 11th Edition keeps the same broad structure as 10th: build armies by points, fight over five battle rounds, and score victory points from missions. Core rules are free on Warhammer Community. Existing 10th Edition codexes remain valid. Major shifts: modular detachments, terrain-based objectives, cover as a hit modifier, a reworked Fight phase, and stickier Battle-shock.

ruleSections.title: Turn Structure
ruleSections.content:
Each player turn follows five phases:

1. Command Phase — Battle-shock tests, stratagems, abilities; scoring sub-step at phase end.
2. Movement Phase — Normal moves, Advances, Falls Back, Remain Stationary; Overwatch at phase end.
3. Shooting Phase — Normal, Assault, Close-quarters, or Indirect shooting modes.
4. Charge Phase — Declare charges within 12", roll 2D6, then move to engage targets.
5. Fight Phase — Pile-in, alternating fights, consolidation.

Battle rounds continue until the mission ends (typically five rounds in matched play).

ruleSections.title: Command Phase
ruleSections.content: The Command Phase has defined start and end steps. After start-of-phase effects, Battle-shocked units and units at or below Half-strength take Leadership tests. Failed tests apply Battle-shock. Other Command abilities resolve in a dedicated step after Battle-shock. Mission scoring at the end of the Command Phase always uses the board state after all other end-of-phase effects.

ruleSections.title: Attack Sequence
ruleSections.content:
When a unit attacks:

1. Select targets in range (and visible, unless Indirect rules apply).
2. Group identical attacks (same BS/WS, S, AP, D, and abilities).
3. Roll hits, then wounds for the group.
4. Defender groups models by save profile; allocates lowest save rolls to non-character groups first, characters last.
5. Apply damage; remove destroyed models. Mortal wounds from the group resolve before the next group.

Repeat for each target unit and weapon group.

ruleSections.title: Shooting Phase
ruleSections.content:
Eligible units shoot unless they Advanced (except Assault weapons), Fell Back, started an Action, or are blocked by other rules.

Shooting modes:
• Normal — did not Advance; not Engaged.
• Assault — Advanced; Assault weapons only.
• Close-quarters — Engaged; Pistols/Close-quarters at engaged foes; Monsters/Vehicles have penalties on non-Close-quarters weapons.
• Indirect — Indirect Fire weapons at non-visible targets with severe hit penalties unless stationary with a spotter.

Cover imposes -1 BS on the attacker. Overwatch occurs at the end of the enemy Movement phase: one unit, visible target within 24", hit on 6+, no re-rolls.

ruleSections.title: Charge & Fight Phases
ruleSections.content:
Charge: eligible units within 12" of enemies roll 2D6, then select target(s) in range and charge move. Engagement range is 2". Successful charges grant Fights First.

Fight: all pile-ins (active player first), then alternate choosing units to fight (active player chooses first Fights First unit). Models fight if within 2" of an enemy. Overrun Fights add a pile-in before attacking when a unit becomes eligible while unengaged. After all fights, units that could fight may consolidate 3" toward enemies or objectives per consolidation mode.

ruleSections.title: Battle-shock
ruleSections.content:
In the Command phase, roll 2D6 for each:
• Unit at or below Half-strength.
• Unit that was Battle-shocked when the phase began.

The test is passed if the result is equal to or greater than the unit's Leadership. If a unit fails, it is Battle-shocked until it passes a later test. While Battle-shocked: Objective Control is "-" (a null value that cannot be modified up), cannot be targeted by Stratagems, cannot start or continue Actions. Insane Bravery (once per game) auto-passes one test on a unit that is not already Battle-shocked.

ruleSections.title: Detachments & Force Dispositions
ruleSections.content: Army construction uses Detachment Points (typically 2 at 1,000 pts, 3 at 2,000 pts) to buy one or more detachments. Each detachment has a point cost, rules, stratagems, enhancements, and a Force Disposition. Dispositions: Take and Hold, Purge the Foe, Reconnaissance, Disruption, Priority Assets. Your chosen disposition and your opponent's set the Chapter Approved mission matchup. See the Munitorum Field Manual and faction packs for detachment costs — not duplicated here.

ruleSections.title: Scoring & Missions
ruleSections.content: Matched play uses the Chapter Approved mission deck. Each player may have different primary objectives from the matchup. Typical caps per player: up to 45 VP from primaries and up to 45 VP from secondaries over the game, with a maximum of 15 VP from primaries and 15 VP from secondaries in any single battle round. Draw two secondary cards in your Command phase each turn and keep unscored cards — there is no hand size limit. Battle Ready awards 10 VP if your army is fully painted. Fixed secondaries (pick two, score up to 20 VP each) remain an option. Twist cards are optional. Full mission text is on physical or digital cards — Tabletome summarizes the system only.

ruleSections.title: Terrain & Objectives
ruleSections.content: Objectives are terrain footprints and marked areas, not separate round markers. Terrain categories include Exposed, Light, and Dense. Dense features use footprints and are often Solid (no line of sight through). Models on a footprint can see and be seen through it with any part of their base on the footprint. Control is measured against the terrain area per mission rules.

ruleSections.title: Cover & Hidden
ruleSections.content: Benefit of Cover: attackers suffer -1 BS when shooting eligible targets in cover (Infantry/Beasts/Swarms in terrain footprints or not fully visible; vehicles/monsters need obscurement). Hidden: eligible Infantry/Beast/Swarm units in a terrain area with at least one Light or Dense feature that have not made ranged attacks this or last turn cannot be targeted from beyond Detection Range (usually 15"). Gone to Ground reduces Detection Range by 3" when partially obscured by Solid terrain. Plunging Fire grants +1 BS from height ≥3".

ruleSections.title: Objective Control (OC)
ruleSections.content: Objective Control measures how strongly a unit holds objectives. Battle-shocked units set OC to "-" — a null value that cannot be increased by abilities. Units need OC 1+ to start most Actions.

ruleSections.title: Armour Penetration (AP)
ruleSections.content: AP subtracts from armour save rolls. Invulnerable saves are checked separately; a wound fails only if both the Armour save and Invulnerable save fail. Invulnerable saves are mandatory — you cannot choose to skip them for a worse armour result.

ruleSections.title: Detachment Points
ruleSections.content: A budget spent during army construction to include detachments. At 2,000 points you usually have three Detachment Points. Detachments cost one to three points. Unused points are wasted.

ruleSections.title: Force Disposition
ruleSections.content: One of five strategic roles tied to detachments: Take and Hold, Purge the Foe, Reconnaissance, Disruption, Priority Assets. Combined with your opponent's disposition, it selects your mission matchup.

ruleSections.title: Hidden
ruleSections.content: A visibility state. Hidden units cannot be targeted by attacks unless the attacking unit is within Detection Range. Indirect Fire can still target them with penalties.

ruleSections.title: Hazardous
ruleSections.content: When a rule calls for a Hazard test, roll D6: on 1-2 the model suffers 1 mortal wound (3 on Monsters/Vehicles). Used by Hazardous weapons, emergency disembark, and Desperate Escape tests.
